sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Hypothermic cardiac arrest is managed according to ANZCOR hypothermia and adult resuscitation guidance. Resuscitation priorities remain CPR, airway and oxygenation support, careful handling, prevention of further heat loss, and rapid access to definitive rewarming capability where appropriate.

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Severe hypothermia can mimic death - always assess carefully; do not declare death until the patient is warm and unresponsive to resuscitation
- "No one is dead until they are warm and dead" - continue resuscitation unless obvious incompatible-with-life injuries are present or rewarming is not achievable
- Handle gently and minimise unnecessary movement - ventricular fibrillation can be provoked by rough handling
- Temperature measurement and rhythm interpretation should integrate with the full clinical picture
- **Osborne (J) waves** on ECG - a positive deflection at the J-point (junction of QRS and ST segment) - are characteristic of hypothermia; they do not require treatment but confirm severity
- Atrial arrhythmias are common in hypothermia and often resolve with rewarming; routine treatment may be harmful

**Temperature thresholds and defibrillation:**
- **Mild (32–35°C):** Defibrillation as per standard ALS
- **Moderate (28–32°C):** Attempt defibrillation; if VF persists after 3 shocks, defer further shocks until core temp >30°C - drugs and further defibrillation have reduced efficacy
- **Severe (<28°C / ≤30°C per some guidelines):** Limit to 3 defibrillation attempts until rewarming is achieved; vasopressors and antiarrhythmics have limited effect and may accumulate to toxic levels
- **Asystole in hypothermia:** CPR indicated; do not use epinephrine routinely until >30°C

**Rewarming hierarchy:**
1. Passive external (remove wet clothing, insulate, warm environment)
2. Active external (warm packs, bear-hugger, MEQU fluid warmer for IV fluids)
3. Active internal (warmed IV fluids, warm humidified oxygen)
4. Extracorporeal rewarming - ECMO or cardiac bypass (definitive care only)

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Start or continue CPR if cardiac arrest is confirmed
- Protect the airway and provide oxygenation or ventilation as required
- Prevent further heat loss with insulation and environmental control
- Avoid rough handling and unnecessary pauses
- Expedite access to definitive care capable of advanced rewarming

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Advanced airway where required for airway protection or oxygenation - intubation does NOT trigger VF if handled gently and core temp >28°C
- Administer warmed IV fluids where available (MEQU fluid warmer) to support rewarming
- Defibrillation strategy based on core temperature (see Considerations)
- Cardiac monitoring: identify VF, atrial arrhythmias, Osborne J waves, QT prolongation
- Do not administer adrenaline or antiarrhythmics routinely until core temperature >30°C
- If VF is refractory to 3 shocks at <30°C, continue CPR and defer further shocks and drugs until rewarming progresses
- Use iSTAT or point-of-care testing where available; pH and potassium levels inform prognosis (K⁺ >12 mmol/L in non-asphyxia arrest = poor prognosis for ECMO)
- Contact retrieval/ECMO service early if extracorporeal rewarming pathway may be relevant
- Expedite to facility with advanced rewarming capability

**This guideline should be read alongside the Hypothermia guideline and the current hypothermic cardiac arrest algorithm.**

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - This guideline should be read together with the hypothermia guideline and the current local hypothermic cardiac arrest chart
